hook: Baroque cathedral on Place Rossetti, named for a martyr who drifted ashore
facts: Built 1650-1685, consecrated 1699; bell-tower added 1757
Dedicated to Saint Reparate, a 3rd-century martyr
Facade dates from 1830
Stands on Place Rossetti in the old town
legend: Legend says Nice fishermen found Reparate's body in a boat steered by a dove and angels, which named the Baie des Anges
w: Rome in miniature on Place Rossetti, raised 1650-1685 over a martyr's cult. Legend says fishermen found young Reparate adrift in a boat piloted by a dove and a flock of angels, which supposedly gave the Baie des Anges its name. Tiled dome, gilt interior, ice-cream queue outside.

hook: Jesuit baroque church on Rue Droite that set the template for the Riviera
facts: First stone laid 1612 by the Jesuits
Modelled on the Gesu in Rome and churches in Turin
Jesuits expelled 1773; became a parish 1802
Classified a historic monument since 1971
w: Funded in 1603 by a Nice merchant who'd made his fortune in Rome, the Jesuits' chapel on Rue Droite went up from 1612 in full Piedmontese baroque. Stucco saints, trompe-l'oeil, gilt by the acre. Its plan would be copied by most baroque churches along the coast. The blueprint, not the copy.

hook: Century-old family wine cellar on Rue de la Prefecture, wine still on tap
facts: Family business since 1910, five generations
At 16 rue de la Prefecture in the old town
Sells local wine on tap from the barrel
Around forty organic wines among great vintages
w: Five generations of Caprioglios have poured here since 1910, and the place looks it: dim, barrel-lined, time stopped somewhere mid-century. Bring your own bottle and they'll fill it from the cask with vin de pays, or sell you a Burgundy older than your grandparents. Old Nice cellar, undimmed.

hook: Nice's oldest grand square, ochre arcades on the old road to Turin
facts: Laid out from 1773 by architect Antoine Spinelli
Built to dignify the royal carriage road to Turin
Renamed for Giuseppe Garibaldi in 1870
Garibaldi was born in Nice in 1807
w: Nice's oldest big square, traced from 1773 to receive carriages off the royal road from Turin, all uniform ochre arcades cribbed from the via Po. It honours Giuseppe Garibaldi, the unifier of Italy, born a few streets away in 1807. His statue glares south toward the country he made. Savoy, in stone.

hook: The last public wash-house of old Nice, tucked off Rue du Malonat
facts: Built 1907 after washhouses elsewhere were demolished
Used by laundresses until 1975
Two basins: one for soap, one for rinsing
On rue du Malonat in the old town
w: Down a side lane off Rue du Malonat sits the last of Nice's public wash-houses, opened 1907 and scrubbed at by bugadieres until 1975. Two stone basins, soap then rinse, where four centuries of gossip and laundry got wrung out together. Washing machines killed it; the troughs remain, mossing over.

hook: A giant square head you can walk inside; it holds a public library
facts: Sculpture by Sacha Sosno, inaugurated 2002
A 30m head of aluminium over a glass cube
Houses offices of the Louis Nucera library
Overlooks Place Yves Klein
w: Sosno called it the first inhabited monumental sculpture on Earth: a 30-metre aluminium head, all jaw and chin, perched on a glass cube that holds the city library's offices. Bureaucrats shuffle paper inside a thinking skull. Inaugurated 2002, staring blankly over Place Yves Klein. Architecture with a face.

hook: Belle Epoque opera house rebuilt after a fire that killed dozens mid-performance
facts: Fire on 23 March 1881 during Lucia di Lammermoor killed 63
Rebuilt by Francois Aune, a pupil of Gustave Eiffel
Plans approved by Charles Garnier
Reopened 1885 with Verdi's Aida
w: On 23 March 1881, a gas footlight set the old theatre ablaze mid-Lucia di Lammermoor; sixty-three died in the crush. The city rebuilt on the ashes, plans by an Eiffel pupil, blessed by Garnier himself, reopening in 1885 with Aida. Belle Epoque gilt and red velvet, raised over a catastrophe.

hook: Former palace of the Savoy dukes and Sardinian kings, now the prefecture
facts: Residence of the Savoy dukes from around 1610 to 1860
Known as the Palais des Rois de Sardaigne
Napoleon III and Tsar Alexander II met here in 1864
Prefecture of the Alpes-Maritimes since 1861
w: For 250 years the Savoy dukes and Sardinian kings held court behind this facade, until 1860 handed Nice to France. Napoleon III and Tsar Alexander II met inside in 1864; now functionaries process passports where royalty once dined. Restorations stripped most of the palace away. Power, rebranded.

n: Musee international d'Art naif Anatole Jakovsky
hook: Naive art in a perfumer's chateau, Coty's old villa above the sea
facts: Housed in the Chateau Sainte-Helene, once perfumer Francois Coty's villa
Opened 1982 from Anatole Jakovsky's gift of 600-plus works
Holds Rousseau, Seraphine Louis, Grandma Moses
Set in a large park west of the centre
w: The perfume baron Francois Coty once lived in this hilltop chateau at Fabron; now it shows the dream-logic of self-taught painters. Critic Anatole Jakovsky gave the city 600-odd works in 1982: Rousseau's jungles, Seraphine's flowers, Grandma Moses. Untrained eyes, in a millionaire's old drawing rooms.
